Farn Polrrrcal PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56ffi o Fax (916) 322-0886
llune 4,2004
Mr. Williain Lepowsky
544 Santa Clara Avenue
Berkeley, CA947O7
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4.-04-096
Dear Mr. Lepowsky:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest and
honoraria provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTTON
Under the Act, may you, a faculty member or other college employee purchase a math
textbook you authored, to use personally or to deliver to another instructor at the college and
receive reimbursement from the college administration for the textbook?
CONCLUSION
The Act does not give rise to a conflict of interest or an impermissible honorarium for
you, a faculty member or other college employee when purchasing a math textbook you
authored, for personal use or for delivery to another instructor at the collegg and receiving
reimbursement forthe textbook from the college administration.
FACTS
You are an instructor at a California community college. You have just completed the
revision of a math textbook you wrote. This textbook has bean adoptd by the faculty of your
math department as the required course textbook and will be used at your college.
The revised textbook will be printed at a local copy store where it will be sold as a reader.
The copy store is the only place where your book may be purchased, and it provides no free
copies of the book. All copies, including desk copies for instructors, must be purchased. You
I Government Code sections S I 000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections I 8 I 09-
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
,6iE-:


FileNo. A-04-096
Page No. 2
will receive a royalty of approximately five or ten dollars on each copy of the textbook which is
purchased.
Should an instructor or the library need a desk copy, a facultymember in your
departrnent will purchase the textbook from the copy store and deliver it to the appropriate
source. You conternplate that perhaps two faculty members per semester will need a copy of the
textbook-
The faculty member will then request reimbursement from the administration for the cost
of the book- The faculty member would thus be a go-between for the copy store (the seller) and
the college (the buyer). There may be two to three such reimbursement requests per year and
reimbursements would come from available college fimds. No facrrltymembermay receive
reimbursernent unless the administration provides its written authorization.
Since 1969, when you began teaching at your college, you have not been required to
submit a Form 700 statement of economic interests.
AI\TALYSIS
Answering your question, we turn to the Act's conflict-of-interest and honoraria
provisions. The Act's conflict-of-interest prcvisions prohibit apublic official ftom making a
governmental decision which will have a material financial effect (xl an economic interest of the
official's. ($ 87100.) The Act also prolu'bits certain public officials and designated employees
from accepting an honorarium, defined as "anypa5rment made in consideration for any speech
$vffi, article published, or attendance at anypublic orprivate conference, @nvention, meeting,
social event meal, or like gathering-" ($$ 89501(a) and 89502.)
1. Conflicts of fnterest Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making,
participating in making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest-
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials," a term defined
to include "everymember, officer, employee or consultant of a strate orlocal government
agency...." ($$82048,87100,87103;reg. 18700(b)(l).) Communitycollegesmewithinthe
Act's definition of a local government agency- (See, e.g., WashingtonAdvice Letter, No. A-02-
O34a; Donato Advice Letter, No. A-96-308; and Willis Advice Ietter, No. I-95-270.) As a
professor at a community college, you are a "public official" and the Act's conflict-of-interest
rules applyto you.
The Act's conflict-of-interest rules apply only where a public official "make[s],
participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his official position to influenie a
govefilmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.


File No. A-04-096
Page No. 3
($ 87100; reg. 18700OX2).) A decision's effect on an official's personal finances is considered
material if it reaches at least $250 in arry l2-month period. (Reg. 1g705.5.)
From the facts you provided, the decision you will be making is selecting your textbook
for use in your class, and deciding to purchase your textbook for your own use or for use by
several other faculty members in the math deparhnent at your college- Although these decisions
could have an effect on your own personal finances because of the fiveto ten dollar royalty you
receive on the sale of each book, such a decision would not give rise to a conflict of inierest-
under the Act, because it falls within an excqrtion in the Act's regulations for certain acadenic
decisions.
Regulation l87U-4 states that *[m]aking orparticipating in making a governmental
decision shall not include:"
T.-.11
"(c) Academic Decisions
(l) Except as provided in subsection (c)(2), neither disclosure of financial
interests nor disqualification is required rmder Governmemt Code sections B7l0O,
87302, or any Conflict of Interest Code, in connection with:
(A) Teaching decisions, including the selection by a teacher ofbooks or other
educational materials for use within his or her own school or instifution, and other
decisions incidental to teaching -.--"
Thus under the Ac! a teacher is permittd to assign his or her ovm textbook for his or
her own classes, or purchase copies for the teacher or faculty's use, even though he or she
would earn $250 or more in royalties from the sale of the textbook. (S* Gtlbeme Advice
Letter, No. 4-87-149.) Under regulation l87L2.a@)(1), your de-sision to use orprnchase
copies of the math textbook you authored, and to seek reimbursement from the
administration for the book, is not considered a govemmental decision and will not give rise
to a conflict-of-interest for you under the Act-
Part of your question concef,ns the appropriate method for requesting and receiving
reimbursement from the college administration when you or staffpurchase copies of the
textbook for your own personal use or that of other faculty members. This uspot of the
question involves administrative procedures at the collegg rather than provisilns ofthe Ac!
and we therefore cannotprovide you with advice-
2. Ifonoraria. The Act's prohibition on honoraria fordesignated employees is
contained in section 89502(c) which states:
"No member of a state board or commission and no designated
eraployee of a state or local government agency shall accept an
honorarium from any source if the member or anployee would be


FileNo. A-04-096
Page No. 4
required to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or
her statement of economic interests-"
An "honorarium" is defined in section 89501 as follows:
"(a) Forpurposes of this chapter, 'honorarium'means, except as
provided in subdivision (b), anypayment made in consideration for any
speech glven, article published, or attendance at any public or private
conferencg convention, meeting social even! meal, orlike gathering
(b) The term 'honorarium' does not include:
(l) Earned income forpersonal services which are customarilyprcvided
in connection with the practice of a bona fide business, tradg or
profession, such as teaching practicing law, medicine, insurance, real
estatg banking orbuilding contracting unless the sole orpredominant
activity of the business, tradg orprofession is making qreeches. ...-
In addition, regulation 18931.2 defines an "articlepublished" as follows:
"(a) Forpurposes of Government Code Sections 89501 through 89506,
an 'article published' means a nonfictional written work:
(l) -'rhat is producd in connection with any activity other than the
practice of a bona fide business, tradg orprofession; and
(2) That is published in a periodical, jorrnal, newqpaper, newsletter,
maganne, pamphlet, or similar publication
(b) ForprxPoses of this regulation, an individual receivespayrnent for
an article published if he or she receives a pa5rment for:
(l) Drafting any part of an article published, except activities solely
involving secretarial assistance.
Q) Berngidentifid as an author of or conhibutor to the article,
(c) 'Article' does not include books, plays, or screenplays.,,
Under the facts you have provided, the payments you receive from the sale of
your math textbook are not prohibited honoraria- You state that you are not a designated
employee who is required to file a statement of economic interests underthe Ac!2 and
therefore the prohibition of section 89502(c) is inapplicable to you-
2 Whether a professor or irstructor is considered a designated employee is dependent on the
conflict of interest code adopted by their college or universit5r. Many times professors are not designated in
their agency's conflict of interest code unless they have significant administrative, purchasing, or
contracting responsibilities. (See Ortiz Advice Letter, No, G43-106.)


File No. A-04-096
Page No. 5
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
By:
Senior Counsel, I*gal Division
HPwjg
